title: extreme_heat_emergency_response
description: A prolonged extreme heat event triggers a mass casualty emergency across a major city. Emergency services must coordinate cooling centres, field triage, hospital surge, and outreach to vulnerable populations simultaneously.

timeline.title: Extreme Heat Warning Issued
timeline.description: Day 4 of heat event. Forecast high of 44°C. Cooling centres activated. EMS surge protocol initiated.

timeline.title: EMS Call Volume Surges
timeline.description: EMS call volume at 300% of baseline. Heat stroke and heat exhaustion reports from across the city.

timeline.title: Hospital Surge — ED at Capacity
timeline.description: All three regional EDs at capacity. Alternate care sites being activated in community centres.

timeline.title: Mass Casualty at Outdoor Event
timeline.description: Outdoor music festival reports 60 attendees with heat illness. MCI declared at venue.

timeline.title: Power Grid Stress — Rolling Outages
timeline.description: Electricity provider warns of potential rolling blackouts. Cooling centres and hospitals on emergency power.

timeline.title: Welfare Check — Vulnerable Residents
timeline.description: Outreach teams conducting welfare checks on isolated elderly residents. Multiple non-responsive individuals found.

node-1.title: Extreme Heat Emergency — Day 4
node-1.situation: A heat wave with temperatures reaching 44°C has entered its fourth consecutive day. EMS call volume is at 300% of baseline with heat stroke and heat exhaustion reports across the city. Cooling centres have been open since Day 1 but are not reaching the most vulnerable. Hospitals are approaching capacity. You are the Emergency Management Director.
node-1.question: What is your immediate priority action?

options.label: A
options.text: Activate the city-wide heat emergency plan: expand cooling centre capacity, deploy mobile outreach teams to isolated elderly and low-income residents, coordinate hospital surge protocols, and issue a public emergency alert with cooling centre locations.

options.label: B
options.text: Focus exclusively on EMS response — field triage and transport is the most acute need.

options.label: C
options.text: Wait to see if temperatures drop in the afternoon before escalating the response.

node-2.title: Field Triage — Heat Illness Differentiation
node-2.situation: A paramedic crew responds to a park where six adults have collapsed. They must differentiate heat exhaustion from heat stroke to determine appropriate treatment. Patient 1: altered consciousness, hot dry skin, core temp 41.5°C. Patient 2: heavy sweating, pale, nauseous, temp 38.8°C, alert. Patient 3: confused, sweating, temp 40.1°C.
node-2.question: How should these patients be triaged and treated?

options.label: A
options.text: Patient 1 — heat stroke (Red): begin immediate aggressive cooling (ice bath/cold packs to neck, groin, axillae) and urgent ALS transport. Patient 3 — heat stroke (Red): same cooling protocol and ALS transport. Patient 2 — heat exhaustion (Yellow): move to shade, oral fluids if alert, IV access, monitor for deterioration.

options.label: B
options.text: Treat all three the same — shade and oral hydration — and transport together.

options.label: C
options.text: Patient 1 has hot dry skin which indicates dehydration — give IV fluids and transport. Patients 2 and 3 are less serious.

node-3.title: Mass Casualty at Outdoor Festival
node-3.situation: An outdoor music festival has reported 60 attendees with heat illness. MCI has been declared. On-site medical staff have set up a treatment tent but are overwhelmed. Triage: 8 suspected heat stroke (Red), 22 heat exhaustion (Yellow), 30 mild (Green). All hospitals are at or near capacity. The venue has a large air-conditioned indoor arena area adjacent to the festival.
node-3.question: How do you manage this MCI?

options.label: A
options.text: Convert the indoor arena into a mass treatment area with on-site cooling; apply aggressive cooling to all Red patients immediately; coordinate with hospitals for the 8 Red patients only (the most critical); treat Yellow patients on-site with IV fluids and active cooling; send Green patients to the cooling area with monitoring.

options.label: B
options.text: Transport all 60 patients to hospitals — on-site treatment is insufficient for this scale.

options.label: C
options.text: Have all patients move to the shade at the festival grounds and wait for additional ambulances.

node-4.title: Power Grid — Rolling Blackout Warning
node-4.situation: The power utility has issued a rolling blackout warning — outages of up to 90 minutes may affect areas of the city, including two cooling centres that are not on hospital-grade backup power. A cooling centre currently sheltering 340 people (including 80 high-risk elderly and medically vulnerable individuals) is in the predicted outage zone.
node-4.question: How do you respond to the blackout warning?

options.label: A
options.text: Pre-emptively evacuate the 80 high-risk individuals from the at-risk cooling centre to a facility with guaranteed backup power; request a portable generator for the cooling centre; coordinate with the utility to designate cooling centres as critical load sites exempt from blackouts.

options.label: B
options.text: Wait for the blackout to occur and respond at that time — it may not happen.

options.label: C
options.text: Evacuate the entire cooling centre population as a precaution.

node-5.title: Welfare Check — Isolated Elderly
node-5.situation: Outreach teams conducting welfare checks have found three isolated elderly residents in their homes: one is unresponsive with a core temp of 41.8°C; one is confused, flushed, and reports not having eaten or drunk in two days; one is mildly confused but can converse and has been using a fan (no air conditioning). All three live alone.
node-5.question: What is the appropriate response for each resident?

options.label: A
options.text: Resident 1 — heat stroke emergency: immediate aggressive cooling and ALS transport. Resident 2 — heat exhaustion with dehydration: IV fluids, cooling, and ALS transport to ED. Resident 3 — at-risk but currently stable: transport to cooling centre, schedule follow-up welfare check, connect with social services for air conditioning support.

options.label: B
options.text: Transport all three to the ED — the hospital can assess each one appropriately.

options.label: C
options.text: Leave residents 2 and 3 with water and instructions to call if they worsen — transport only Resident 1.

node-6.title: Scenario Complete
node-6.situation: All eight heat stroke patients from the festival were aggressively cooled and transported — seven survived. Resident 1 was resuscitated and recovered with early aggressive cooling. The 80 high-risk individuals were evacuated before the blackout, which lasted 75 minutes. Mobile outreach teams conducted 214 welfare checks during the event. A post-event review identified gaps in reaching non-English-speaking and disabled residents, driving updates to the heat emergency plan.
